sections.content: A Search and Rescue Transponder (SART) operates in the 9 GHz radar band. When interrogated by a nearby ship's or aircraft's X-band radar, the SART responds with a signal that appears as a distinctive pattern on the radar screen, guiding rescuers directly to the survivor's position.

rows: 12 equally spaced dots extending outward from the SART position
Approximately 8 NM by ship radar, 30 NM by aircraft
Minimum 96 hours standby + 8 hours continuous operation
At least 1 metre above sea level for best radar detection

sections.content: As the rescuing vessel approaches, the 12 dots merge into 12 concentric arcs, and finally into complete circles when very close. This changing pattern confirms you are closing on the SART position.

sections.prompt: How does a SART appear on a ship's radar?

options.text: 12 equally spaced dots in a line from the SART position
A flashing ring around the radar center
A continuous line pointing toward the SART

sections.explanation: A SART produces 12 equally spaced dots on the radar display, extending outward from the SART's position. As you get closer, these dots become arcs and then full circles.
